coredata.dc:title: Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 capped gold nanoclusters as an “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for sensitive detection of MnO4 − and ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Zhiqiang
Liao, Yuanping
Wu, Huiyan
Xiao, Qi
Liu, Yi
Yang, Liyun
coredata.dc:description: Herein, zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 capped gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@ZIF-8) was fabricated and developed as an “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for selectively and sensitively detecting KMnO4 and ascorbic acid (AA). The prepared AuNCs@ZIF-8 initially exhibited strong fluorescence at 595 nm KMnO4 could quench the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8, rendering the fluorescence signal of AuNCs@ZIF-8 in the “off” state. The probe allows KMnO4 to be detected with a 10 nM detection limit and the linear analytical range was 0.05–2 μM. When AA was added into the AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 system, the fluorescence of the AuNCs@ZIF-8 recovered due to the redox reaction between KMnO4 and AA, and the fluorescence signal of the system turned “on”. The detection limit of AA was 37 nM in a good linear concentration range of 0.05–5 μM. Furthermore, this AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe was successfully used for AA detecting in fresh orange juices with the recoveries ranged from 96.6% to 108.2%, which validates the potential applicability of this strategy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ascorbic acid
Gold nanoclusters
Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8
Fluorescent detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of KMnO4 by AuNCs@ZIF-8 sensor
Detection of AA by AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 sensor
Detection of AA in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Nature Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22163004 ), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province ( 2021GXNSFBA220060 ), Guangxi Scientific and Technological Development Projects ( Guike AD20159068 , Guike AD20297042 ), BAGUI Scholar Program of Guangxi Province of China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of AuNCs@ZIF-8. (a) TEM image of AuNCs@ZIF-8; (b) Size distribution of AuNCs@ZIF-8; (c) Fluorescence and UV–vis spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8, the inset corresponds to the digital pictures taken under sunlight and a 365 nm UV lamp; (d) X-ray diffraction patterns of AuNCs@ZIF-8 and simulated ZIF-8 (JCPDS 00-062-1030).
Excitation and emission spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8 and UV–vis spectrum of KMnO4.

The influences of the concentration of AuNCs@ZIF-8 (a) and reaction time (b) on the fluorescence quenching ability (F 0/F) of KMnO4 on nanoprobe, F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of KMnO4，respectively.

(a) The influence of the concentration of KMnO4 on the fluorescence quenching ability (F 0/F) on AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe; (b) The linear relationship between the concentration of KMnO4 and F 0/F, F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of KMnO4，respectively.

(a) The influence of the concentration of KMnO4 on F 0/F value; (b) Changes in the F 0/F value of the sensing system containing AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 and AA (0, 1, 3, 5, 10 μM) during different time durations, F 0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 in the absence and presence of AA, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 sensing system in the presence of different concentrations of AA; (b) The linear relationship between the concentration of AA and F/F 0, F 0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 in the absence and presence of AA，respectively.
Selectivity of the AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 system for AA analysis.
Schematic illustration of detection strategy for KMnO4 and AA based on AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe.
Comparison of methods for the determination of AA.
Determination of AA in fresh orange juices.

head.ce:title: Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 capped gold nanoclusters as an “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for sensitive detection of MnO4 − and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning, 530001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Chemistry, Tiangong University, Tianjin, 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Chemistry, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning, 530001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 capped gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@ZIF-8) was fabricated and developed as an “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for selectively and sensitively detecting KMnO4 and ascorbic acid (AA). The prepared AuNCs@ZIF-8 initially exhibited strong fluorescence at 595 nm KMnO4 could quench the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8, rendering the fluorescence signal of AuNCs@ZIF-8 in the “off” state. The probe allows KMnO4 to be detected with a 10 nM detection limit and the linear analytical range was 0.05–2 μM. When AA was added into the AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 system, the fluorescence of the AuNCs@ZIF-8 recovered due to the redox reaction between KMnO4 and AA, and the fluorescence signal of the system turned “on”. The detection limit of AA was 37 nM in a good linear concentration range of 0.05–5 μM. Furthermore, this AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe was successfully used for AA detecting in fresh orange juices with the recoveries ranged from 96.6% to 108.2%, which validates the potential applicability of this strategy.

An “on-off-on” fluorescence strategy based on AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite for consecutively detection of MnO4− and ascorbic acid.
Image 1

ce:keyword.ce:text: Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 capped gold nanoclusters

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the oxidizing ability of permanganate (MnO4 −), low concentration of MnO4 − is widely used in sterilization, anti-infection, treatment of skin itching, etc [1]. However, high concentration of MnO4 − is harmful to human health and is irritating and corroding to human skin and tissue [2]. Thus, the sensitive detection of MnO4 − is important and shows great necessity.

Ascorbic acid (AA), also known as Vitamin C, naturally exists in vegetables and fruits. AA is an essential nutrient required for various biological processes in human body and has been widely used in medicines, food and cosmetics [3]. Due to its excellent antioxidant properties, AA can remove oxidative free radicals and protect human body from oxidative stress [4]. AA can also act as cofactor participating in some important enzymatic reactions [5]. In addition, it performs vital function in maintenance of immune function [6], iron absorption [7], hormone production [8], etc. The lack of ascorbic acid can cause scurvy, ulcers, loose teeth, fragile bones, bone disease, low disease resistance and pseudoparalysis of the lower limbs [9]. However, the abuse of AA directly leads to diarrhea, kidney stones and stomach cramps, etc [10,11]. Therefore, the specific and accurate determination of AA is of great importance for human health and food safety.

So far, various methods for detecting AA have been developed, including iodometry [12], titration analysis [13], high performance liquid chromatography [14], electrochemistry [15], spectrophotometry [16], fluorescence analysis [17] et al. Due to the superiority in low cost, simple instruments operation and sample preparation, time-saving, fluorescence analysis has been widely used in the detection of AA.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), composed of metal ions and organic ligand, have attracted great attentions in the past decade. The large specific surface area, high porosity, unique pore structure, adjustable structure and variable chemical composition of MOFs make them promising candidates in fields of gas storage, catalysis, separation, sensing and biomedicine [18,19]. Recently, MOFs have been adopted for the construction of nanoprobe by the incorporation of nanomaterials in the cavities of MOFs [20]. The adjustability of the structure and porosities of MOFs makes it accessible to realize the bury of nanomaterials, enhancing the stability of nanomaterials. Furthermore, the pore structure helps the enrichment of analyte from testing samples, improving the sensitivity and selectivity of nanoprobe [21]. By encapsulating CdTe quantum dots (QDs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8), Wang et al. constructed a size-selective fluorescent nanosensor (CdTe@ZIF-8) for the selective monitoring of oxidase activities. H2O2 produced from the enzymatic reaction between urate oxidase (UOx) and uric acid (UA) could permeate the cavities of ZIF-8 and quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs, while the permeation of UOx is obstructed due to its larger size [22]. Based on Eu-MOFs capped carbon dots (CDs@Eu-MOFs), Guo et al. constructed a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for detecting Hg2+. Owning to the enrichment effect of Eu-MOFs for Hg2+, the quenching ability of Hg2+ on CDs@Eu-MOFs is greatly enhanced [23]. Wu et al. prepared CDs encapsulated ZIF-90 (CDs@ZIF-90) and applied it to the fluorescence-scattering sensing of phosphate. Upon the addition of PO4 3−, the structure of ZIF-90 decomposes, leading to the fluorescence enhancement of CDs and the diminution of second-order scattering [24]. Kim et al. developed MOF-derived ZnO/CuO (M-ZnO/CuO) nanoprobe, the probe with 40 mol% CuO exhibited good sensing performance (response, selectivity, and repeatability) for H2S [25].

Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), composed of several or dozens of gold atoms, show promising application prospects in research fields, including environmental monitoring, food safety and disease diagnosis [26]. The ulstra-small size, good biocompatibility, large stokes shift and excellent photostability of AuNCs make them ideal for the construction of nanosensor.

11-Mercaptoundecanoic acid (11-MUA), ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), dimethylimidazole (2-Mel), selenium powder, zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), cysteine (L-Cys), arginine (Ary), methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), asparagine (Asn), lysine (Lys), glutamic acid (Glu), citric acid (CA) and uric acid were purchased from Aladdin. Potassium hypermanganate (KMnO4), KIO3, K2Cr2O7, NaHCO3, KBr, NaCl, H2O2, NaClO and AgNO3 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company. All reagents are of analytical grade and require no further purification.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained on a RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU), the emission and excitation slit were both 10 nm. The UV–vis spectra were measured using a UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of AuNCs@ZIF-8 were acquired using a JEM-1200EX instrument (JEOL). The specific surface area and pore size distribution of AuNCs@ZIF-8 was obtained by using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method and the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model from the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, which were recorded by a physisorption analyzer (Micromeritics ASAP 2020, USA) at 77 K.

The fabrication of ZIF-8 was based on the literature with some modifications [22]. Briefly, 2-methylimidazole (11.3500 g) was completely dissolved in an appropriate amount of ultra-pure water. Then, 2 mL of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O solution (0.1463 g/mL) was added to generate the preparation of ZIF-8 under stirring at room temperature. After 24 h, nano-sized ZIF-8 was obtained and purified by centrifugation process. This centrifugation process was carried out for three times and the finally obtained precipitation was collected and redispersed in ultra-pure water.

11-MUA (8.7 mg) was added into the purified ZIF-8 solution, followed by the addition of 2-methylimidazole, glutathione, chloroauric acid and sodium hydroxide. After stirring for 11 h, AuNCs@ZIF-8 with strong red fluorescence was obtained. The AuNCs@ZIF-8 was purified through centrifugation and this process was carried out for three times.

The detection of KMnO4 was conducted as follows: 20 μL of AuNCs@ZIF-8 (1.02 mg/mL) was added into 2 mL of Hepes buffer. Subsequently, KMnO4 with various concentrations (0–4.5 μM) was added into AuNCs@ZIF-8 solution. The fluorescence spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8 were collected after KMnO4 was incubated with AuNCs@ZIF-8 for 1 min. The calibration curve for KMnO4 detection was established according to the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 at 595 nm in the existence of varying concentrations of KMnO4. Finally, the LOD of AuNCs@ZIF-8-based probe for KMnO4 detection was calculated using the formula (3 × σ)/κ.

The selectivity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 towards KMnO4 was investigated under the optimum conditions. AuNCs@ZIF-8-based probe was respectively incubated with 4 μM KMnO4 and 20 μM interfering substances, including IO3 −, K2Cr2O7, H2O2, Br−, Cl−, CH3COOH, NaNO3, NaHCO3 and NaClO. The fluorescence spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8 were then measured.

The fluorescence detection of AA was realized by adopting AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 system. KMnO4 could efficiently quench the intrinsic fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8, the addition of AA consumes the existed KMnO4 and enhances the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8. Before monitoring AA, KMnO4 was added into the AuNCs@ZIF-8 solution to establish AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 system. Subsequently, different concentrations of AA varying from 0 to 10 μM were added in this system and their corresponding fluorescence spectra were collected. The LOD for AA detection was calculated according to (3 × σ)/κ. Under the optimum conditions, the selectivity of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 system for AA was evaluated by comparing the fluorescence recovery of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 in the presence of 5 μM AA and 25 μM interfering substances (GSH, UA, CA, Cys, Arg, Asn, Lys, Met, Glu, Phe, glucose, sucrose, K+, Na+, Ca2+ and Mg2+), respectively.

To investigate the applicability of AuNCs@ZIF-8 based nanoprobe for AA detection in real samples, different brands of orange were purchased from local supermarket (Nanning, Guangxi, China) and their corresponding AA content was measured using AuNCs@ZIF-8-based nanoprobe. In the pretreated process, the fresh oranges were pressed in a pestle and mortar to obtain orange juices. The obtained juices were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and diluted 10-fold with HEPES buffer solution to prepare the sample solution. For the detection of AA in fresh orange juices, 20 μL sample solution was added into the solution of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 nanoprobe, the fluorescence spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 nanoprobe were collected and AA content in fresh orange juices was calculated.

The AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe was fabricated through the incorporation of AuNCs into the cavities of ZIF-8 using ship-in-bottle strategy. Briefly, ZIF-8 was pre-synthesized through a self-assembly process at room temperature, precursors for preparing AuNCs were added subsequently and introduced into the cavities of ZIF-8. During the reduction process, chloroauric acid was reduced to AuNCs and AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe was obtained. The AuNCs@ZIF-8 was purified by centrifugation for three times. AuNCs emit strong red fluorescence, while ZIF-8 is non-emissive. Once AuNCs are incorporated in the structure of ZIF-8, red fluorescence could be observed in the precipitation of the purification process. Thus, the red fluorescence of precipitation is a strong predictor of the formation of AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe. Fig. S1 shows the digital pictures of the solution of precipitation purified for different times. The three solutions all show strong red fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp, indicating the successful incorporation of AuNCs in ZIF-8. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 centrifuged for different times seldom changes, meaning AuNCs are incorporated in the cavities of ZIF-8 rather than absorbed on the surface of ZIF-8. Furthermore, UV–vis spectra, fluorescence spectra, XRD and TEM measurements were carried out to identify the property and structure of AuNCs@ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 1 a, AuNCs@ZIF-8 exhibits good dispersibility and regular dodecahedron structure. The average size of AuNCs@ZIF-8 is 47.5 ± 17.0 nm (Fig. 1b), indicating the formation of nano-sized AuNCs@ZIF-8. Fig. 1c depicts the fluorescence and UV–vis spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8. The prepared AuNCs@ZIF-8 possesses fluorescence peak at 595 nm, which is ascribed to the incorporated AuNCs. Meanwhile, AuNCs exhibit strong absorbance peak at 241 nm (Fig. S2), while the characteristic peak of AuNCs@ZIF-8 around 250 nm weakens，indicating the bury of AuNCs in the cavities of ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 1d，all major XRD phase peaks of the AuNCs@ZIF-8 match the peaks of simulated ZIF-8, suggesting the purity and crystallinity of the synthesized AuNCs@ZIF-8 [27]. Fig. S3 displays characteristic Type I isotherms, indicating the microporous structure of AuNCs@ZIF-8. Additionally, the pore size distribution shows that there are micropores in AuNCs@ZIF-8. The BET surface area of AuNCs@ZIF-8 was obtained as 339.8 m2/g, much lower than the reported BET surface area of ZIF-8, which is ascribed to the incorporated AuNCs in the cavities of ZIF-8. Based on the above results, it is definite that fluorescent AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite material was successfully prepared.

UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra were applied to investigate the mechanism for KMnO4 detection. Fig. 2 depicts the UV–vis spectrum of KMnO4 and excitation and emission spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8. KMnO4 possesses two strong absorption bands (270–400 nm and 450–600 nm). The maximum excitation for AuNCs@ZIF-8 is 284 nm and AuNCs@ZIF-8 depicts absorption band from 230 to 360 nm. AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe exhibits strong red fluorescence at 595 nm. Meanwhile, the UV–vis spectrum of KMnO4 and excitation and emission spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8 show relatively great overlap, meaning the strong fluorescence quenching ability of KMnO4 on AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe, which is ascribed to the inner-filter effect between KMnO4 and AuNCs@ZIF-8 [28].

The quenching mechanism can be expressed as dynamic quenching or static quenching [29]. Different quenching mechanism can be classified by quenching constants at different temperatures. For dynamic quenching, the quenching constants tend to increase with temperature increasing. In contrast, a lower quenching constant can be obtained at higher temperature for static quenching. The Stern-Volmer equation can be used for describing the change of fluorescence intensity at different temperatures:
(1)
F 0/F = 1+K sv[Q]
Where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of KMnO4, [Q] is the concentration of the KMnO4, and K sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.
As illustrated in Fig. S4, the quenching results agree well with the Stern-Volmer equation. The K sv value decreases with the increase of temperature, indicating the static quenching mechanism.

To investigate the mechanism for detecting AA, UV–vis measurement of KMnO4 in the presence of AuNCs@ZIF-8 and AA were conducted. As shown in Fig. S5, the absorption peak of KMnO4 locates at 545 nm, while AA and AuNCs@ZIF-8 seldom show absorption around 545 nm. Once AA is added, the characteristic peak of KMnO4 disappears, which is ascribed to the consumption of KMnO4 by AA through redox reaction.

The above results indicate that KMnO4 quenches the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8 by inner-filter effect and static quenching, while AA eliminates the fluorescence quenching effect of KMnO4 on AuNCs@ZIF-8 through redox reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of KMnO4 by AuNCs@ZIF-8 sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 sensor for KMnO4 detection could be affected by the concentration of AuNCs@ZIF-8, the interaction time between KMnO4 and AuNCs@ZIF-8 and the buffer solution. Thus, it is necessary to explore the optimal detection conditions for detecting KMnO4.

Fig. 3 a depicts the fluorescence quenching effect of KMnO4 on AuNCs@ZIF-8. F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity at 595 nm in the absence and presence of KMnO4, respectively. The value of F 0/F elevates with the increasing concentration of AuNCs@ZIF-8 in the range of 0–10.20 μg/mL, while the value of F 0/F shows a downward trend when the concentration of AuNCs@ZIF-8 is higher than 10.20 μg/mL, meaning that the optimal concentration of AuNCs@ZIF-8 for KMnO4 detection is 10.20 μg/mL. At the optimal concentration of AuNCs@ZIF-8, the detection time of AuNCs@ZIF-8 for KMnO4 is explored. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the value of F 0/F is relatively low and remained stable for a long time, indicating the good stability of AuNCs@ZIF-8. Once the addition of KMnO4, the value of F 0/F increases quickly and reaches its maximum in several seconds, demonstrating that the time required for detecting KMnO4 and stabilizing the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 is extremely short. Fig. S6 illustrates the effect of buffer solution on the quenching ability of KMnO4 on nanoprobe. It is obvious that the AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe shows low response to KMnO4 in Tris-HCl and PBS solution, while KMnO4 could effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe in water and HEPES solution. As AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe is designed for the detection of AA in food and physiological conditions, HEPES is chosen as buffer solution for KMnO4 and AA detection in subsequent experiments.

Under the optimal detection conditions, the sensitivity for KMnO4 detection was explored by the addition of various concentrations of KMnO4 into the solution of AuNCs@ZIF-8. As the concentration of KMnO4 increases, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 gradually decreases (Fig. 4 a), indicating that KMnO4 can strongly quench the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8. When the added concentration of KMnO4 is 2 μM, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of AuNCs@ZIF-8 is 70%. While the fluorescence quenching efficiency of AuNCs is 32% at the same concentration of KMnO4 (Fig. S7), implying that the cavities structure of ZIF-8 could improve the response ability of AuNCs for KMnO4, which is ascribed to the enrichment of the cavities structure of ZIF-8. The fluorescence quenching ability (F 0/F) shows good linear relationship with the concentration of KMnO4 in the range of 0.05–2 μM (Fig. 4b). From the formula β = (3 × σ)/κ, the LOD of KMnO4 is calculated to be 10 nM, which is competitive with the LOD of most reported fluorescent nanoprobe (Table S1).

In the selectivity experiment for detecting KMnO4, substances with certain oxidizing ability are mainly selected as interferent. As shown in Fig. 5 , KMnO4 could effectively quench the fluorescence AuNCs@ZIF-8, which is ascribed to inner-filter effect and static quenching of KMnO4 on AuNCs@ZIF-8. The fluorescence quenching ability of interfering substances on AuNCs@ZIF-8 is significantly weaker than that of KMnO4, indicating the good selectivity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 for KMnO4 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA by AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 sensor for AA detection may be affected by the concentration of KMnO4 and the detection time. In the sensing system, KMnO4 acts as bridge for the detection of AA, and its concentration can be a key influence factor for the detecting effects. When the concentration of KMnO4 is high, the minimum concentration of AA required to restore the fluorescence of the sensor will increase. When the concentration of KMnO4 is low, it could not effectively quench the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8, which leads to the low recovery rate of the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8. Therefore, the concentration of KMnO4 must be optimized to ensure the high sensitivity of the sensor. Fig. 6 a depicts the optimization result of KMnO4 concentration. When the concentration of KMnO4 varies in the range of 0.5–4.0 μM, the F/F 0 ratio increases with the increasing concentration of KMnO4. When the KMnO4 concentration is higher than 4 μM, the ratio of F/F 0 decreases, indicating that the optimal KMnO4 concentration for detecting AA is 4 μM. Under the optimal concentration of KMnO4, the interaction time for AA detection was explored and the results are shown in Fig. 6b. Obviously, the value of F/F 0 is relatively low and remained stable for a long time before the addition of AA. Once AA was added, the ratio of F/F 0 increases and reaches its maximum after about 11 min, meaning the best detection time is 11 min.

Under the optimal detection conditions, various concentrations of AA were added into the AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 solution, the fluorescence spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 were measured and collected. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 at 595 nm increases with the introduction of AA, which is ascribed to the elimination of KMnO4 through the redox reaction between KMnO4 and AA. when the AA concentration is in the range of 0.05–5 μM, the F/F 0 and AA concentrations possess a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.992). The LOD of AA detection is 0.037 μM. Compared with most reported fluorescent nanosensor (Table 1 ), AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 based sensor for AA detection shows better LOD.

To further examine the selectivity of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 for AA, the influence of carbohydrates, amino acids and possible coexisting substances (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, etc) was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 , AA can effectively restore the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8, while other interfering substances show lower fluorescence recovery ability on AuNCs@ZIF-8, indicating the good selectivity of this sensor for AA detection, which is ascribed to the strong reducing ability of AA. The oxidizing ability of KMnO4 highly depends on the pH of the solution. Under neutral condition, the redox potential of KMnO4 is about 0.58 V, much lower than its redox potential (1.49 V) in acidic condition. Thus, the oxidizing ability of KMnO4 is inhibited in HEPES solution and AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 shows high selectivity for strong reductant (AA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the applicability of AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4 system for real sample analysis, the content of AA in fresh orange juice samples was tested and the results were shown in Table 2 . The recovery rate is between 96.6% and 108.2%, which indicates that the detection method can be used for the detection of real samples.

In summary, we proposed a novel “on-off-on” fluorescence strategy for selective and sensitive detection of KMnO4 and AA. AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe was fabricated through the incorporation of AuNCs into the cavities of ZIF-8 using the ship-in-bottle strategy. In the sensing system, AuNCs acts as signal response group, while ZIF-8 could enrich analyte and improve the sensitivity of nanoprobe. Based on the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs@ZIF-8 caused by fluorescence inner filter effect and static quenching between KMnO4 and AuNCs@ZIF-8, AuNCs@ZIF-8 could realize the selective detection of KMnO4. Upon the addition of AA to AuNCs@ZIF-8/KMnO4, the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8 restored, which is ascribed to the consumption of KMnO4 by AA. Benefiting from the enrichment of the cavities structure of AuNCs@ZIF-8 for analytes, the sensitivity for AA detection is improved.

Zhiqiang Zhou: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Yuanping Liao: Investigation, Data curation. Huiyan Wu: Resources. Qi Xiao: Formal analysis. Yi Liu: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Liyun Yang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Nature Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22163004), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2021GXNSFBA220060), Guangxi Scientific and Technological Development Projects (Guike AD20159068, Guike AD20297042), BAGUI Scholar Program of Guangxi Province of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.micromeso.2022.111971.
